addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + byte throughput, binary rate via a
     reg-75 read or `0xFA` sniff. Resolve the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] here. (current)
   - 0.3.0-2 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-3 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-4 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1	# Bugs
2	
3	This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
4	defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
5	describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.
6	
7	Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
8	permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
9	never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
10	anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
11	title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.
12	
13	Next Issue #: 3
14	
15	## Bugs
16	
17	### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART
18	
19	A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
20	the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
21	afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
22	by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
23	per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
24	baud.
25	
26	The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
27	command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
28	connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
29	**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.
30	
31	- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
32	  between switches):
33	    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
34	      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
35	    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
36	      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
37	    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
38	      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
39	      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
40	- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
41	  baud, explicit `--baud`):
42	    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
43	      clean, 40.3 Hz.
44	    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
45	    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
46	      40.0 Hz.
47	    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
48	      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
49	      115200 / 40 Hz.
50	- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
51	  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
52	- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
53	  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
54	  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
55	  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
56	  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
57	- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
58	  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
59	  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
60	  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
61	  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
62	  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
63	  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
64	  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
65	  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
66	- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
67	  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
68	  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
69	  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.
70	
71	**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
72	that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
73	ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
74	power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
75	200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
76	higher device baud.
77	
78	### Issue #2 — `read_reply` closure parameter named `matches` collides with the `matches!` macro
79	
80	In `read_reply` (`src/main.rs`), the predicate closure parameter is
81	named `matches`, while the same function body uses the std `matches!`
82	macro to classify a read error kind. Two unrelated things share the
83	name in one scope:
84	
85	- `matches!(e.kind(), ErrorKind::TimedOut | ErrorKind::WouldBlock)` —
86	  the standard-library macro (note the `!`), a pattern test on the
87	  I/O error.
88	- `matches(&candidate)` — the caller-supplied `FnMut(&str) -> bool`
89	  closure that decides whether an assembled reply line is the one
90	  we're waiting for. It is **not** an exact-equality test: every
91	  call site passes a `starts_with` *prefix* predicate (e.g.
92	  `|l| l.starts_with("$VNWRG,75")`), and `transact` further OR's in
93	  a `$VNERR` prefix so a device-error reply also terminates the read
94	  (`read_reply(port, deadline, |l| accept(l) || l.starts_with("$VNERR"))`).
95	  `read_reply` slices `candidate` from the last `$` before testing,
96	  so the prefix is anchored to the real reply, not stray leading
97	  bytes.
98	
99	The closure threads through `transact_retry -> transact -> read_reply`
100	as `impl Fn(&str) -> bool`. A reader scanning `read_reply` sees the
101	`matches!(...)` macro first and reasonably assumes the later
102	`matches(...)` is the same thing, when the actual closure invocation
103	is the prefix-based line-acceptance test.
104	
105	- Proposed fix: rename the parameter (and its doc-comment mention) to
106	  `is_acceptable` — it is a function parameter and call sites are
107	  unaffected (the closure is passed positionally).
108	- Pure readability defect: behavior is correct; the name collision
109	  costs reader time and invites misreading.
110	
111	**Cost / why it matters:** no runtime impact, but `read_reply` is the
112	core of every command's reply path, so the confusion tax is paid by
113	anyone tracing how `GetHZ` (or any transact) reads a reply.
114	
115	# References
116	
117	[1]: chores/chores-01.md#rs-232-link-analysis
118

# Bugs

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.

Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.

Next Issue #: 3

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
baud.

The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.

- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
  between switches):
    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
  baud, explicit `--baud`):
    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
      clean, 40.3 Hz.
    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
      40.0 Hz.
    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
      115200 / 40 Hz.
- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.

**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
higher device baud.

### Issue #2 — `read_reply` closure parameter named `matches` collides with the `matches!` macro

In `read_reply` (`src/main.rs`), the predicate closure parameter is
named `matches`, while the same function body uses the std `matches!`
macro to classify a read error kind. Two unrelated things share the
name in one scope:

- `matches!(e.kind(), ErrorKind::TimedOut | ErrorKind::WouldBlock)` —
  the standard-library macro (note the `!`), a pattern test on the
  I/O error.
- `matches(&candidate)` — the caller-supplied `FnMut(&str) -> bool`
  closure that decides whether an assembled reply line is the one
  we're waiting for. It is **not** an exact-equality test: every
  call site passes a `starts_with` *prefix* predicate (e.g.
  `|l| l.starts_with("$VNWRG,75")`), and `transact` further OR's in
  a `$VNERR` prefix so a device-error reply also terminates the read
  (`read_reply(port, deadline, |l| accept(l) || l.starts_with("$VNERR"))`).
  `read_reply` slices `candidate` from the last `$` before testing,
  so the prefix is anchored to the real reply, not stray leading
  bytes.

The closure threads through `transact_retry -> transact -> read_reply`
as `impl Fn(&str) -> bool`. A reader scanning `read_reply` sees the
`matches!(...)` macro first and reasonably assumes the later
`matches(...)` is the same thing, when the actual closure invocation
is the prefix-based line-acceptance test.

- Proposed fix: rename the parameter (and its doc-comment mention) to
  `is_acceptable` — it is a function parameter and call sites are
  unaffected (the closure is passed positionally).
- Pure readability defect: behavior is correct; the name collision
  costs reader time and invites misreading.

**Cost / why it matters:** no runtime impact, but `read_reply` is the
core of every command's reply path, so the confusion tax is paid by
anyone tracing how `GetHZ` (or any transact) reads a reply.

## feat: passive bench, composable command grammar

Commits:

Today's `bench` is too complex: every run *mutates* the device
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and each subcommand is its own process — one port
open per invocation, and each fresh-process reopen is a wedge
die-roll [[1]]. The redesign makes `bench` purely passive, breaks
configuration into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, runs a whole
CLI line over a single connection, and adds file-backed named
states. We think running the line over one open cuts the
reconnect count that Issue #1 ties to wedges.

- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
  port once and runs its steps in order. See
  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
  output mode. See
  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).

content.content:
100	  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
101	- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
102	  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).
103	
104	### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps
105	
106	The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
107	grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
108	space handling.
109	
110	- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
111	- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
112	  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
113	  single device write.
114	- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
115	  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
116	  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
117	  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
118	  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
119	- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
120	  apply, then measure.
121	- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
122	  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
123	  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
124	  restore, all on one open.
125	
126	### Universal rate (option A)
127	
128	`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
129	tool maps it to the right register.
130	
131	- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
132	- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
133	  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
134	  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
135	  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
136	- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
137	  device's current divisor.
138	
139	### Named states (file-backed) + default
140	
141	The `--config` file (default `./.rdwr_vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
142	hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
143	key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:
144	
145	- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
146	  NAME (or the default name).

[Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).

### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps

The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
space handling.

- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
  single device write.
- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
  apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
  device's current divisor.

### Named states (file-backed) + default

The `--config` file (default `./.rdwr_vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
  NAME (or the default name).
- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
  no device read.
- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
  name) *to* the device.
- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
  instead.

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,
  matches today's bench), with `--persist` available.
- `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device mid-line — constrain
  them to standalone / last.
- Passive binary frame-rate needs a frame length: reuse
  `get-bin`'s reg-75 parser to self-configure, or sniff the
  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.


## fix: binary output targets the wrong VN-100 serial port on TTL

Commits: [[4]]

First contact with the VN-100 from `rdwr_vn100` on the RPi5 flight
target succeeded over the 3.3 V TTL header UART (`/dev/ttyAMA0`,
RP1 UART0 on GPIO14/15, `dtoverlay=uart0-pi5` loaded). ASCII paths
work bare; binary output needed a fix and surfaced a real frame
budget limit. Both are measured below.

- Read paths over TTL at 115200 are clean: `get-hz` returns
  40 Hz; the ASCII async `bench` measures 39.9 Hz on a full
  `$VNYMR` stream (~42% link use), no checksum/framing errors.
- `bench --bin` returned "0 frames" until the binary-output port
  was corrected — the tool hardcodes register-75 `asyncMode=1`,
  but the Pi's TX/RX link answers to port 2. See
  [register-75 serial-port numbering on TTL](#vn-100-register-75-serial-port-numbering-on-ttl).
- The full attitude+IMU set does not fit at 200 Hz / 115200: the
  device rejects it with a `$VNERR` 0x0C. See
  [200 Hz binary frame budget at 115200](#200-hz-binary-frame-budget-at-115200).
- The flight target needs only `/dev/ttyAMA0`; the tool's default
  `--port` is still the bench `/dev/ttyUSB0` (`main.rs:463`),
  passed explicitly on the Pi. Making the Pi port the default is
  an open ergonomics question, tracked in todo.

### VN-100 register-75 serial-port numbering on TTL

The VN-100 binary-output register (75) names a target serial port
in its first field (`asyncMode`): 1, 2, or 3 (both). ASCII async
(registers 6/7) instead targets whichever port the config command
arrived on, so it needs no port number and "just works" on the
connected wire — which is why `get-hz` and the ASCII `bench`
succeeded immediately. `rdwr_vn100` hardcodes `asyncMode=1`
(`main.rs:1060`, `bench_binary`), which on the Pi's wire emits no
binary at all.

Measured on `/dev/ttyAMA0`, identical field set (time+accel,
200 Hz), 2 s raw capture via `../fc/scripts/serial_check.py read`:

- `asyncMode=1` → 9760 B, ASCII `$VNYMR` only, no `0xFA` frames.
- `asyncMode=2` → 20145 B, `0xFA` binary frames present.
- `asyncMode=3` → 20151 B, `0xFA` binary frames present.

So the Pi's serial port (its one TX/RX pair on GPIO14/15) is the
VN-100's serial port **2** in the register-75 numbering; the bench
RS-232 rig used port 1.

Port 1 and port 2 are two *separate physical UART interfaces* on
the VN-100 — different pin sets — not two logical streams sharing
one line. We are wired to exactly one of them (port 2); port 1 is
the interface the bench RS-232 transceiver used, and the Pi is not
connected to it at all. What *is* interleaved on our one port is
two message *types*, not two ports:

- ASCII async (`$VNYMR`, registers 6/7) targets whichever port the
  config arrived on, so it lands on port 2 (us) and is always
  present on our line.
- Binary async (the `0xFA` frames, register 75) carries an
  explicit port-number field. With `asyncMode` 2 or 3 the binary
  is also directed to port 2, so our line then carries ASCII and
  binary time-multiplexed; with `asyncMode=1` the binary goes out
  port 1 (the interface we are not wired to), so we see ASCII only.

The byte counts confirm this is two types on one port, not two
ports: ASCII-only is ~4.9 kB/s (40 Hz YMR); the mode-2 / mode-3
captures add ~5.2 kB/s of binary (200 Hz × 26 B) on the *same*
line, doubling the total to ~10 kB/s. We think the VN-100 exposes
two UART interfaces with the RS-232 transceiver on UART1 and the
Pi's GPIO14/15 TTL lines on UART2 — inferred from the port-number
behavior, not from a pinout we have verified.

The fix is to stop hardcoding port 1: expose a
`--serial-port {1,2,both}` choice and pick a default that suits
the flight target (port 2, or `both` for robustness). The default
is a design decision for the fix cycle.

### 200 Hz binary frame budget at 115200

At 200 Hz the per-sample period is 5 ms; at 115200 baud 8N1
(11520 B/s) that is a raw budget of 57.6 bytes on the wire. The
VN-100 applies a more conservative internal margin than the raw
budget:

- time+accel (26 B/frame, 20 B payload) → accepted, streams clean
  at 199.9 Hz (~45% link use).
- accel+gyro (30 B/frame, 24 B payload) → accepted, streams clean
  at 199.9 Hz (~52% link use).
- time+accel+gyro (38 B/frame, 32 B payload) → rejected with
  `$VNERR` 0x0C ("insufficient baud rate").
- time+accel+gyro+quat (54 B/frame, 48 B payload) → rejected.

So the usable binary frame at 200 Hz / 115200 sits between 30 B
(accepted) and 38 B (rejected) — well under the 57.6 B raw budget,
i.e. the VN-100 keeps a large internal margin. In practice that is
time plus *one* 3-axis vector, or two vectors without time; a
third 12-byte vector busts it. Carrying time + accel + gyro (the
IMU essentials), let alone a quaternion, at 200 Hz needs a higher
baud — which reopens the high-baud reconnect wedge question
([[1]], Issue #1) on TTL this time, where we think it may not
reproduce.

1	# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): the project's generated
8	   artifact — code, prose, image, song, whatever it produces.
9	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
10	
11	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
12	
13	**Committing vs pushing.** Default to `vc-x1 push` — it commits and
14	publishes both repos together, each carrying one `ochid:` pointing at
15	the other (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol) and
16	[ochid trailers](#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)). Use a bare
17	`jj commit` (see [jj Basics](#jj-basics)) only when:
18	
19	- the change will never be published — no `ochid:`;
20	- the commit will be squashed away before its series is pushed
21	  (loop-and-squash) — no `ochid:`; it disappears in the squash;
22	- the commit will be pushed later as a non-top commit — `jj commit`
23	  and add its `ochid:` now, because `vc-x1 push` stamps only the
24	  topmost commit it pushes, never the ancestors.
25	
26	**What "commit" and "push" mean.** In an instruction, "commit",
27	"push", and "commit + push" all mean `vc-x1 push` — land *and*
28	publish — unless stated otherwise. A bare `jj commit` is asked for
29	by name: "local commit", "save locally", "just `jj commit`" (the
30	cases above). So the spoken default is publish; the local-only
31	save is the named exception. The approval around a push —
32	interactive by default, waived only by an explicit scoped
33	delegation — is the cycle protocol's
34	[Pushing policy](notes/cycle-protocol.md#policy).
35	
36	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
37	
38	- App repo: `.` (project root)
39	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
40	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
41	
42	## Working Directory
43	
44	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
45	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
46	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
47	command failures downstream.
48	
49	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
50	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
51	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
52	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
53	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
54	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
55	not a stylistic choice.
56	
57	Run **one command per shell invocation** — don't bundle several
58	steps into a single compound script (`a && b; c`). Bundling hides
59	which step produced which output and makes a failure ambiguous;
60	executing one at a time keeps the details visible and reviewable.
61	Exceptions are a genuine pipeline (`grep | sort`) or a tight,
62	inseparable pair where the join is the point.
63	
64	## File reads — read the slice you need
65	
66	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
67	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
68	
69	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
70	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
71	  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
72	  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
73	  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
74	  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
75	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
76	  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
77	  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
78	  first.
79	- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
80	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
81	  todo.md.
82	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
83	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
84	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
85	  the section you need.
86	
87	**Why:** the routine read should stay small. `notes/todo.md`
88	grows every cycle, so the backlog and bugs live in sibling
89	files rather than inline; the same "slice you need" rule
90	applies to historical files.
91	
92	## Memory
93	
94	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
95	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
96	AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
97	and it loses on discoverability:
98	
99	- **AGENTS.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
100	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
101	  (human or bot).
102	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
103	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
104	
105	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
106	durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
107	
108	## Speculation marker
109	
110	Durable text the bot writes — AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
111	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
112	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
113	enters the text, prefix it with "We think ..." (a royal "we") so a
114	reader can tell the measured from the inferred.
115	
116	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
117	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
118	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
119	in the written record.
120	
121	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
122	marker. Prefix with "We think ..." (or a close variant like
123	"Our guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
124	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
125	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
126	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
127	
128	## jj Basics
129	
130	**Use jj, not git, for version-control operations** (status, log,
131	diff, commit, push, history rewrite). jj coexists with the git
132	backend, so the repo *can* be driven with raw `git`, but this
133	project's workflow — bookmarks, the working-copy `@` model, ochid
134	trailers — is expressed in jj terms; reaching for `git` invites
135	state that doesn't match the jj documentation here. There is no
136	`jj mv`: to move/rename a tracked file, just `mv` it on disk and
137	jj detects the rename by content.
138	
139	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
140	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
141	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
142	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
143	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
144	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
145	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
146	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
147	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
148	  session because session data updates continuously.
149	- `jj rebase` uses `--onto`/`-o` to name the destination(s).
150	
151	## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)
152	
153	The cross-reference between the app repo and the `.claude` bot
154	repo is what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
155	counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
156	"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
157	**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.
158	
159	A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
160	survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
161	which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
162	counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:
163	
164	- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the app repo
165	  (vc-template-x1). `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo.
166	- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.

16

18	  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
19	  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
20	  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).

22	  version, in the project's manifest (see
23	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
24	  a built or running artifact derives from it.
25	- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.
26	
27	## Recording the version-of-record
28	
29	Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
30	surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
31	your medium:
32	
33	- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
34	  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
35	  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
36	    committed config it's sourced from)
37	  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
38	    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
39	    needed
40	- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
41	  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
42	    store it verbatim
43	  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
44	    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
45	    version, just the stored spelling
46	- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
47	  version-of-record:
48	  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
49	  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
50	- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
51	  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
52	  this project follows the per-commit preference.
53	
54	## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)
55	
56	Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
57	on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
58	the exact commit it came from.
59	
60	- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
61	  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
62	  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
63	  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
64	  if it differs.
65	- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
66	  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
67	  contain (see the cycle protocol's
68	  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).
69	
70	## Step numbering
71	
72	The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
73	[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
74	phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
75	a Preparation**:
76	
77	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
78	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
79	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
80	
81	**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
82	no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
83	Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
84	needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
85	grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
86	renumbering siblings.
87	
88	Disambiguation:
89	
90	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
91	  Preparation.
92	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
93	  identifier `0`).
94	
95	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
96	
97	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
98	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
99	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
100	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
101	
102	Bump the version-of-record at the start of each phase, so the
103	active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,
104	every commit carries a distinct version.

# Versioning

How this project versions its commits and the running
artifact. The scheme — the cycle step-numbering and the
unique-per-commit aim — is generic and shared across projects:
this file is copied **verbatim**, with
[Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
covering each medium by conditional rather than per-project
edits.

## Terms

Three names, used as defined here across
[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md),
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md), and the notes files:

- **version** — the per-commit version (e.g. `0.3.0-5.3.0`),
  written with `-` in prose, ladders, chores, todo, and commit
  talk; its suffix encodes the cycle phase (see
  [Step numbering](#step-numbering)).
- **version-of-record** — the authoritative stored copy of the
  version, in the project's manifest (see
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record));
  a built or running artifact derives from it.
- **versioning** — the topic: this scheme as a whole.

## Recording the version-of-record

Where the version-of-record lives, how it's stored and
surfaced, and how often it changes — pick the case that fits
your medium:

- **Manifest** — where the version-of-record is stored:
  - if Rust, `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
  - if Python, `pyproject.toml` `[project].version` (or the
    committed config it's sourced from)
  - otherwise wherever the medium records it (a generic
    `version.toml`, a book's frontmatter, …) — add the case as
    needed
- **Notation** — how the `-` form is stored:
  - if the format allows `-` (TOML `version.toml`, `Cargo.toml`),
    store it verbatim
  - if it bars `-` (PEP 440's local segment, e.g. a Python
    project), remap to `+` — `0.3.0-5.3.0` → `0.3.0+5.3.0`: same
    version, just the stored spelling
- **Reporter** — how a built artifact surfaces the
  version-of-record:
  - if a CLI app, `<cli-app> -V`
  - if a TUI/GUI, add to Help/About or display on the title
- **Cadence** — how often to bump: see
  [Unique per commit](#unique-per-commit-preference-not-requirement);
  this project follows the per-commit preference.

## Unique per commit (preference, not requirement)

Our general notion is that the version-of-record should change
on **every commit**, so a built or running artifact identifies
the exact commit it came from.

- This is a preference, **not** a hard requirement. A project
  following the cycle protocol may bump less often — once per
  cycle, only at release, and so on. Record the choice in
  [Recording the version-of-record](#recording-the-version-of-record)
  if it differs.
- It is achievable because the cycle's versions (below) are
  **pre-assignable**, unlike the git SHA, which a commit cannot
  contain (see the cycle protocol's
  [Commits backfill](cycle-protocol.md#commits-backfill)).

## Step numbering

The cycle (Preparation -> Work -> Close-out; see
[cycle-protocol.md](cycle-protocol.md)) encodes each commit's
phase in the version suffix — the **final identifier `0` marks
a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

**Preparation is optional.** A lightweight cycle — no ladder,
no setup commit — skips `-0` and starts at `-1` (its first
Work commit). The same holds at every level: a sub-cycle
needing no Preparation omits its `.0` (see Nesting). One that
grows a Preparation later adds the `0` step without
renumbering siblings.

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

active phase is recorded — and, per the preference above,

Writing the passive scanner is straightforward, but removing the two mutating bench functions orphans a cluster of config helpers, and the project builds with `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` — so dead code fails the build. This needs your call because it shapes the diff and touches the clean-tree norm:

Removing `bench_binary`/`bench_ascii` leaves **~8 config helpers with no live caller** — `parse_reg06`, `parse_serial_port`, `parse_ascii_type`, `ascii_type_name`, `parse_fields`, `default_fields`, `lookup_field`, `field_names` (+ their tests). **0.3.0-2 reintroduces essentially all of them** for the `get/set-ascii` (reg 6) and `get/set-bin` (reg 75) verbs.

(The passive decoder still keeps `FIELDS`, `decode_binary_sample`, `rd_f32`, `vn_crc16`, `is_async_line` — those stay live.)

content.text:
Good two-part push — let me take them in turn.

**Reversibility.** Nothing here needs `jj undo` of history: every change so far is uncommitted working-copy state. The script only rewrites `src/main.rs`, so reversing is `jj restore src/main.rs` (snaps it back to the parent commit), and `jj diff` shows the full change for review before we keep anything. And right now it's moot — the script was rejected, so `src/main.rs` is still untouched; the only applied changes are the version bump (`Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock`) and the chores backfill. Your underlying point stands, though: a 200-line scripted splice is hard to *review*, even if it's easy to revert. I'd show you `jj diff` before accepting it regardless.

**The lib split.** I agree — `main.rs` is 1890 lines and 0.3.0 is about to pile on a lot (get/set verbs, the `+`-step grammar, a TOML config, named states). Splitting *before* that build-out is the right time, so the new surface lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith. A natural layout:

- `proto.rs` — VN-100 primitives (`checksum`, `vn_crc16`, `build_command`, `verify_checksum`, `error_description`, reg parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`)
- `transact.rs` — port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`, `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`)
- `bench.rs` — the new passive bench
- `cli.rs` — `parse_args`, `Command`, `help_text`
- `main.rs` — thin: parse → open port → dispatch; `lib.rs` ties it together

But that's a structural refactor that deserves its **own cycle** — mixing "move 1500 lines into modules" with "change bench behavior" makes the 0.3.0-1 diff unreviewable, and it's exactly the kind of thing a single-purpose commit keeps clean. So I'd sequence it as: pause the 0.3.0 ladder, do a refactor cycle (pure code-move, no behavior change, easy to verify the binary is identical), then resume 0.3.0-1 passive bench on the modular tree.

How do you want to play it?

- **A (recommended)** — refactor to a lib first as its own cycle, then passive bench into `bench.rs`.
- **B** — land passive bench in `main.rs` now (reviewable Edits, not the script), refactor later.
- **C** — do the lib split as a sub-cycle inside 0.3.0 right here.

And on the mechanics for whichever we pick: want me to drop the scripted-splice approach in favor of incremental, reviewable `Edit` calls (with `jj diff` checkpoints), or are you fine with a generated edit as long as I show the diff?

The win is the module boundaries (`proto` / `transact` / `bench` / `cli`), and a lib crate is just the vehicle that makes them `pub`-addressable and unit-testable.

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + byte throughput, binary rate via a
     reg-75 read or `0xFA` sniff. Resolve the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] here. (current)
   - 0.3.0-2 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-3 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-4 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the

     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (current)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,

  them to standalone / last.

  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.

1	//! `rdwr_vn100` — read and configure a VectorNav VN-100 IMU over serial.
2	//!
3	//! The VN-100 speaks an ASCII protocol over a serial port. Each command is
4	//!
5	//!     $<payload>*XX\r\n
6	//!
7	//! where `XX` is the 8-bit XOR checksum of every character of `<payload>`
8	//! (i.e. everything between `$` and `*`). Replies echo the same form; binary
9	//! outputs (register 75) use a packed frame ending in a 16-bit CRC instead.
10	//!
11	//! Commands implemented here:
12	//!   get-hz / set-hz       read/write the async output rate    (register 7)
13	//!   baud                  change the serial baud rate          (register 5)
14	//!   rrg / wrg             generic read/write of any register   ($VNRRG / $VNWRG)
15	//!   bench                 configure an output (ASCII async by default, or a
16	//!                         binary output with --bin) and measure the achieved
17	//!                         rate, to see what fits a given baud
18	//!   reset / factory-reset reboot / restore defaults            ($VNRST / $VNRFS)
19	//!
20	//! Key VN messages:
21	//!   $VNRRG,<id>*XX          -> $VNRRG,<id>,<f1>,...*YY   (read register)
22	//!   $VNWRG,<id>,<f1>,...*XX  -> echo                     (write register)
23	//!   $VNWNV*XX               -> $VNWNV*YY                 (save all to flash)
24	//!   $VNERR,<code>*XX                                     (error; see error_description)
25	//!
26	//! The async output rate (register 7) is one of a fixed firmware set
27	//! (`VALID_RATES`); a value that's out of range — or too much data for the
28	//! current baud — returns a `$VNERR` (0x0C = insufficient baud rate).
29	//!
30	//! Register/enum/table values are cited to the ICD and vnsdk in REFERENCE.md.
31	
32	use std::io::{Read, Write};
33	use std::time::{Duration, Instant};
34	
35	/// Frequencies (Hz) the VN-100 accepts for the async data output rate.
36	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 7" (ICD Reg 7; vnsdk AsyncOutputFreq::Adof).
37	const VALID_RATES: &[u32] = &[1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200];
38	
39	/// Serial baud rates the VN-100 supports (register 5).
40	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 5" (ICD Reg 5; vnsdk BaudRate::BaudRates).
41	const VALID_BAUDS: &[u32] = &[
42	    9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600,
43	];

import re, pathlib

lib = pathlib.Path("src/lib.rs")
lines = lib.read_text().splitlines(keepends=True)

def idx(pred, start=0):
    for i in range(start, len(lines)):
        if pred(lines[i]):
            return i
    raise SystemExit("anchor not found")

def doc_start(i):
    """Walk up over contiguous /// and #[ lines above index i."""
    s = i
    while s-1 >= 0 and re.match(r'\s*(///|#\[)', lines[s-1]):
        s -= 1
    return s

# --- Range A: Field doc .. parse_reg06 closing brace -------------------------
a0 = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('/// A selectable binary-output field'))
pr06 = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('fn parse_reg06'))
a1 = idx(lambda l: l.rstrip('\n') == '}', pr06)
# include trailing blank
a_end = a1 + 1
if lines[a_end].strip() == '':
    a_end += 1
blockA = lines[a0:a_end]

# --- Range B: vn_crc16 doc .. closing brace ----------------------------------
crc = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('fn vn_crc16'))
b0 = doc_start(crc)
b1 = idx(lambda l: l.rstrip('\n') == '}', crc)
b_end = b1 + 1
if lines[b_end].strip() == '':
    b_end += 1
blockB = lines[b0:b_end]

# --- Split the test module into proto vs lib tests ---------------------------
PROTO_TESTS = {
    "checksum_matches_known_value", "builds_read_command", "builds_write_command",
    "verifies_good_checksum", "rejects_bad_checksum", "decodes_insufficient_baud_error",
    "decodes_other_error_codes", "parses_read_and_write_responses",
    "fields_parse_orders_by_bit_and_dedups", "vn_crc16_append_yields_zero",
    "builds_reset_commands",
}
tmod = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('#[cfg(test)]'))
mod_open = idx(lambda l: l.lstrip().startswith('mod tests'), tmod)
use_super = idx(lambda l: 'use super::*;' in l, mod_open)
body0 = use_super + 1
# the test mod closes at the final line `}` of the file
body1 = len(lines) - 1  # last line is the closing `}` of mod tests

proto_tests, lib_tests = [], []
i = body0
while i < body1:
    if lines[i].strip().startswith('#[test]'):
        j = i + 1
        # find this test fn's name then its closing `    }` (exactly 4 spaces)
        name = None
        k = j
        while name is None:
            m = re.match(r'\s*fn (\w+)', lines[k]); k += 1
            if m: name = m.group(1)
        end = idx(lambda l: l.rstrip('\n') == '    }', i)
        block = lines[i:end+1]
        (proto_tests if name in PROTO_TESTS else lib_tests).append(block)
        i = end + 1
    else:
        i += 1

# --- Build proto.rs ----------------------------------------------------------
def pubify(block):
    out = []
    for l in block:
        l = re.sub(r'^(fn |struct |const )', r'pub \1', l)
        out.append(l)
    return out

protoA = pubify(blockA)
protoB = pubify(blockB)
# Field struct fields -> pub
fixed = []
for l in protoA:
    if re.match(r'^    (name|bit|size): ', l):
        l = '    pub ' + l[4:]
    fixed.append(l)
protoA = fixed

header = (
    "//! VN-100 wire-protocol primitives.\n"
    "//!\n"
    "//! - ASCII command framing: `checksum`, `build_command`, `verify_checksum`.\n"
    "//! - Replies / errors: `parse_reg06`, `parse_reg07`, `error_description`,\n"
    "//!   `vnerr_message`.\n"
    "//! - Binary output: `vn_crc16` and the `Field` / `FIELDS` field vocabulary.\n\n"
)
proto = [header]
proto += protoA
proto.append("\n")
proto += protoB
proto.append("\n#[cfg(test)]\nmod tests {\n    use super::*;\n\n")
for n, blk in enumerate(proto_tests):
    proto += blk
    if n != len(proto_tests)-1:
        proto.append("\n")
proto.append("}\n")
pathlib.Path("src/proto.rs").write_text("".join(proto))

# --- Rebuild lib.rs: remove proto blocks + proto tests, add mod, main->run ---
# Remove by slicing from highest index to lowest to keep indices valid.
# Collect removal ranges: blockA [a0,a_end), blockB [b0,b_end), each proto test.
removals = [(a0, a_end), (b0, b_end)]
# proto test ranges (recompute against current lines)
i = body0
while i < body1:
    if lines[i].strip().startswith('#[test]'):
        j = i + 1; name=None; k=j
        while name is None:
            m = re.match(r'\s*fn (\w+)', lines[k]); k += 1
            if m: name = m.group(1)
        end = idx(lambda l: l.rstrip('\n') == '    }', i)
        if name in PROTO_TESTS:
            r0 = i
            # swallow one trailing blank line after the test
            r1 = end + 1
            if r1 < len(lines) and lines[r1].strip() == '':
                r1 += 1
            removals.append((r0, r1))
        i = end + 1
    else:
        i += 1

for (s, e) in sorted(removals, reverse=True):
    del lines[s:e]

text = "".join(lines)
text = text.replace(
    "use std::time::{Duration, Instant};\n",
    "use std::time::{Duration, Instant};\n\nmod proto;\nuse proto::*;\n",
    1,
)
text = text.replace(
    "fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {",
    "/// Parse args, open the serial port, and dispatch the requested command.\npub fn run() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {",
    1,
)
lib.write_text(text)

# --- Thin main.rs ------------------------------------------------------------
pathlib.Path("src/main.rs").write_text(
    "//! Binary entry point for `rdwr_vn100` — delegates to the library crate.\n"
    "//!\n"
    "//! All logic lives in the `rdwr_vn100` library (`lib.rs` and its modules);\n"
    "//! this binary only forwards to `run`.\n\n"
    "/// Forward to the library's `run`, surfacing its error to the process exit.\n"
    "fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {\n"
    "    rdwr_vn100::run()\n"
    "}\n"
)

print("proto.rs:", len(proto), "line-parts;", len(proto_tests), "proto tests")
print("lib tests kept:", len(lib_tests))

content.content:
vc-x1 0.65.2-0

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (current)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- fix: bench silences async before binary config [[4]]

import re, pathlib

lib = pathlib.Path("src/lib.rs")
lines = lib.read_text().splitlines(keepends=True)

def idx(pred, start=0):
    for i in range(start, len(lines)):
        if pred(lines[i]):
            return i
    raise SystemExit("anchor not found")

# --- Block: read_reply doc .. send_reboot_command closing brace --------------
a0 = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('/// Read from the device until'))
srb = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('fn send_reboot_command'))
a1 = idx(lambda l: l.rstrip('\n') == '}', srb)
a_end = a1 + 1
if lines[a_end].strip() == '':
    a_end += 1
block = lines[a0:a_end]

# --- Proto tests for transact -------------------------------------------------
T_TESTS = {
    "read_reply_recovers_reply_after_binary_junk",
    "read_reply_honors_deadline_on_newlineless_garbage",
}
tmod = idx(lambda l: l.startswith('#[cfg(test)]'))
use_super = idx(lambda l: 'use super::*;' in l, tmod)
body0 = use_super + 1
body1 = len(lines) - 1

def collect_tests(want):
    out, i = [], body0
    while i < body1:
        if lines[i].strip().startswith('#[test]'):
            k = i + 1; name = None
            while name is None:
                m = re.match(r'\s*fn (\w+)', lines[k]); k += 1
                if m: name = m.group(1)
            end = idx(lambda l: l.rstrip('\n') == '    }', i)
            if name in want:
                out.append((i, end))
            i = end + 1
        else:
            i += 1
    return out

test_ranges = collect_tests(T_TESTS)
test_blocks = [lines[s:e+1] for (s, e) in test_ranges]

# --- Build transact.rs --------------------------------------------------------
pub_block = [re.sub(r'^(fn )', r'pub \1', l) for l in block]
header = (
    "//! Serial transaction helpers — send a command and read its reply.\n"
    "//!\n"
    "//! - `read_reply` does deadline-bounded raw reads so unmatched garbage\n"
    "//!   can't block forever.\n"
    "//! - `transact` / `transact_retry` send a command and validate the reply\n"
    "//!   (checksum, `$VNERR`); `send_reboot_command` tolerates a missing echo.\n\n"
    "use std::io::{Read, Write};\n"
    "use std::time::{Duration, Instant};\n\n"
    "use crate::proto::{verify_checksum, vnerr_message};\n\n"
)
out = [header] + pub_block
out.append("\n#[cfg(test)]\nmod tests {\n    use super::*;\n\n")
for n, blk in enumerate(test_blocks):
    out += blk
    if n != len(test_blocks)-1:
        out.append("\n")
out.append("}\n")
pathlib.Path("src/transact.rs").write_text("".join(out))

# --- Rewrite lib.rs -----------------------------------------------------------
removals = [(a0, a_end)]
for (s, e) in test_ranges:
    r1 = e + 1
    if r1 < len(lines) and lines[r1].strip() == '':
        r1 += 1
    removals.append((s, r1))
for (s, e) in sorted(removals, reverse=True):
    del lines[s:e]

text = "".join(lines)
text = text.replace(
    "mod proto;\nuse proto::*;\n",
    "mod proto;\nmod transact;\nuse proto::*;\nuse transact::*;\n",
    1,
)
lib.write_text(text)
print("transact.rs written;", len(test_blocks), "tests moved")
PY

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (current)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

                l = 'pub ' + l
        for n in names_struct:

        for n in names_enum:

1	//! Command-line surface — argument parsing, the `Command` enum, and help.
2	//!
3	//! - `parse_args` turns argv into a `Config` (port/baud) plus a `Command`.
4	//! - `help_text` renders usage; the ASCII-type and serial-port helpers map
5	//!   CLI names to register values.
6	
7	use crate::proto::*;
8	
9	/// Frequencies (Hz) the VN-100 accepts for the async data output rate.
10	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 7" (ICD Reg 7; vnsdk AsyncOutputFreq::Adof).
11	const VALID_RATES: &[u32] = &[1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 40, 50, 100, 200];
12	
13	/// Serial baud rates the VN-100 supports (register 5).
14	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 5" (ICD Reg 5; vnsdk BaudRate::BaudRates).
15	const VALID_BAUDS: &[u32] = &[
16	    9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200, 128000, 230400, 460800, 921600,
17	];
18	
19	/// ASCII async message presets (register 6 ADOR): CLI name -> register value.
20	/// Authoritative: REFERENCE.md "Register 6" (ICD §3.2.3 Table 3.6;
21	/// vnsdk AsyncOutputType::Ador).
22	const ASCII_TYPES: &[(&str, u8)] = &[
23	    ("off", 0),
24	    ("ypr", 1),
25	    ("qtn", 2),
26	    ("qmr", 8),
27	    ("mag", 10),
28	    ("acc", 11),
29	    ("gyr", 12),
30	    ("mar", 13),
31	    ("ymr", 14), // the factory default
32	    ("yba", 16),
33	    ("yia", 17),
34	    ("imu", 19),
35	    ("dtv", 30),
36	    ("hve", 34),
37	];
38	
39	fn ascii_type_names() -> String {
40	    ASCII_TYPES
41	        .iter()
42	        .map(|(n, _)| *n)
43	        .collect::<Vec<_>>()
44	        .join(", ")
45	}
46	
47	/// Resolve a `--type` name (case-insensitive, optional `vn` prefix) to its ADOR.
48	fn parse_ascii_type(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
49	    let lower = s.trim().to_lowercase();
50	    let key = lower.strip_prefix("vn").unwrap_or(&lower);
51	    ASCII_TYPES
52	        .iter()
53	        .find(|(n, _)| *n == key)
54	        .map(|(_, v)| *v)
55	        .ok_or_else(|| {
56	            format!(
57	                "unknown ASCII type `{s}`; choose from {}",
58	                ascii_type_names()
59	            )
60	        })
61	}
62	
63	/// Parse the `--serial-port` value into a register-75 `asyncMode` code.
64	///
65	/// - `1` / `2` target one of the VN-100's two UARTs; `both` (or `3`) targets
66	///   both. The default elsewhere is `2`, the RPi5 TTL header (the flight
67	///   target); the RS-232 bench is port 1. Selecting a port also subjects it to
68	///   the register-75 fit check, so `both` is avoided as a default — a port left
69	///   at a low baud would veto a frame the connected port could take.
70	fn parse_serial_port(s: &str) -> Result<u8, String> {
71	    match s.trim().to_lowercase().as_str() {
72	        "1" => Ok(1),
73	        "2" => Ok(2),
74	        "3" | "both" => Ok(3),
75	        other => Err(format!(
76	            "invalid --serial-port `{other}`; choose 1, 2, or both"
77	        )),
78	    }
79	}
80	
81	/// Display name for an ADOR value, e.g. 8 -> "VNYMR".
82	pub fn ascii_type_name(value: u8) -> String {
83	    ASCII_TYPES
84	        .iter()
85	        .find(|(_, v)| *v == value)
86	        .map(|(n, _)| {
87	            if *n == "off" {
88	                "off".to_string()
89	            } else {
90	                format!("VN{}", n.to_uppercase())
91	            }
92	        })
93	        .unwrap_or_else(|| format!("ADOR {value}"))
94	}
95	
96	pub struct Config {
97	    pub port: String,
98	    pub baud: u32,
99	}
100	
101	pub enum Command {
102	    Help,
103	    Version,
104	    GetHz,
105	    SetHz {
106	        hz: u32,
107	        persist: bool,
108	    },
109	    SetBaud {
110	        baud: u32,
111	        persist: bool,
112	    },
113	    Reset,
114	    FactoryReset,
115	    /// Read any register (generic ASCII Read Register).
116	    Rrg {
117	        id: u8,
118	    },
119	    /// Write any register (generic ASCII Write Register).
120	    Wrg {
121	        id: u8,
122	        params: Vec<String>,
123	    },
124	    /// Configure an output (ASCII async by default, or binary with `--bin`) and
125	    /// measure the achieved rate, then restore prior state.
126	    ///
127	    /// - `serial_port` is the register-75 `asyncMode` (binary only): 1 or 2 for
128	    ///   one of the VN-100's two UARTs, 3 for both. Ignored for the ASCII bench.
129	    Bench {
130	        binary: bool,
131	        hz: u32,
132	        secs: u64,
133	        fields: Vec<&'static Field>,
134	        serial_port: u8,
135	        ascii_type: Option<u8>,
136	    },
137	}
138	
139	/// Format one help row: a `label` and one-or-more wrapped description lines,
140	/// aligned to a common description column. If the label is too wide, the
141	/// description starts on the next line.
142	fn help_row(label: &str, desc: &[&str]) -> String {
143	    const COL: usize = 24; // label column width; description starts after it
144	    let mut out = String::new();
145	    if label.len() <= COL {
146	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label:<COL$}{}\n", desc[0]));
147	    } else {
148	        out.push_str(&format!("  {label}\n"));
149	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", desc[0]));
150	    }
151	    for cont in &desc[1..] {
152	        out.push_str(&format!("  {:<COL$}{}\n", "", cont));
153	    }
154	    out
155	}
156

160	    s.push_str("Usage: rdwr_vn100 [--port PORT] [--baud BAUD] <command> [args]\n\n");

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**feat: passive bench, composable command grammar**

Today's `bench` mutates the device on every run
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and reopens the port per subcommand — each reopen
a wedge die-roll. Redesign `bench` to be purely passive,
decompose config into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, run a
whole CLI line over a single connection, and add file-backed
named states. Full design + rationale in chores-01 [[1]].

A code reorganization comes first: `main.rs` (~1890 lines) is
split into a lib crate of focused modules, so the redesign's
large new surface (verbs, step grammar, TOML config, named
states) lands in clean modules instead of growing the monolith.
Design + module layout in chores-01 [[5]].

   - 0.3.0-0 prep: land the design note + this entry. (done)
   - 0.3.0-1 refactor: add `lib.rs` + thin `main.rs`; move the
     VN-100 protocol primitives (checksum/CRC, command framing,
     register parsers, `Field`/`FIELDS`) to `proto.rs`. (done)
   - 0.3.0-2 refactor: move port I/O (`read_reply`, `transact`,
     `transact_retry`, `send_reboot_command`) to `transact.rs`.
     (done)
   - 0.3.0-3 refactor: move arg parsing, the `Command` enum, and
     help text to `cli.rs`; scaffold `bench.rs`. (current)
   - 0.3.0-4 passive bench: `bench [SECS]` measures the live
     stream only — drop the configure/measure/restore code;
     ASCII line-count + binary frame rate via a `0xFA` sniff
     (CRC-checked) + total wire throughput. Resolves the passive
     binary-rate open question [[1]] (0xFA sniff). Lands in
     `bench.rs`.
   - 0.3.0-5 decompose config verbs: add `get-ascii`/`set-ascii`
     (reg 6) and `get-bin`/`set-bin` (reg 75) beside the
     existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`; bare-enable semantics.
   - 0.3.0-6 step grammar + one connection: shell-word steps,
     `+` token join, single port open, left-to-right execution,
     option-A resolve (merge `set-bin`+`set-hz` into one reg-75
     write).
   - 0.3.0-7 named states: `--config` TOML profile map;
     `save-state` / `set-state` / `restore-state`; default =
     bare-`restore-state` target, never auto-applied; baud
     excluded from restore.
   - 0.3.0 close-out: README + `--help` rewrite, validation
     (cargo cycle), version bump.

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: default RPi5 UART, fix binary port on TTL [[2]],[[3]]
- fix: bench silences async before binary config [[4]]
